RiskFactors.full_evidence: Timelock present but execute() function lacks access control - allows permissionless execution after delay
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that timelock present but execute() function lacks access control - allows permissionless execution after delay. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that timelock present but execute() function lacks access control - allows permissionless execution after delay is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Permissionless execution of privileged operations
cir_explanation.summary: Functions like execute(), updateOracle(), or setParameters() lack onlyOwner/onlyAdmin modifiers, allowing any address to call them. Sonne Finance ($20M, 2024) was exploited via a permissionless timelock execute() that an attacker front-ran.

guidance.investor: Permissionless execution of privileged functions means anyone can trigger state changes the protocol intended to restrict. This can enable front-running attacks, oracle manipulation, or parameter hijacking. Prefer protocols where all state-changing functions have explicit access controls.
guidance.developer: Apply onlyOwner or role-based access control to execute() and any function that modifies oracle state, collateral parameters, or protocol configuration. Route governance execution through a TimelockController with restricted executors. Add a Guardian role that can veto during the delay period.

RiskFactors.description: No domain separation found - missing chainId/emitter binding
RiskFactors.full_evidence: No domain separation found - missing chainId/emitter binding
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that no domain separation found - missing chainid/emitter binding. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that no domain separation found - missing chainid/emitter binding is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Missing or incomplete domain separation
cir_explanation.summary: Signed payloads omit `block.chainid` or `address(this)` from the EIP-712 domain separator, so a message signed for Ethereum mainnet can be replayed on Arbitrum or a fork.

guidance.investor: Missing domain separation means signatures are portable between chains and contracts. This is a critical bridge vulnerability that expands the attack surface to every deployment of the same code.
guidance.developer: Use EIP-712 structured signatures with `DOMAIN_SEPARATOR = keccak256(name, version, chainId, verifyingContract)`. Recompute on chain ID change (hard fork protection).

RiskFactors.description: No replay protection found - messages can be replayed
RiskFactors.full_evidence: No replay protection found - messages can be replayed
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that no replay protection found - messages can be replayed. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that no replay protection found - messages can be replayed is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: No replay protection on bridge messages
cir_explanation.summary: No `consumed` mapping or nonce tracking exists on the message execution path. Any valid signed payload can be resubmitted indefinitely.

guidance.investor: This is a critical vulnerability. Without replay protection, a single valid withdrawal proof can be used to drain the entire bridge treasury. Do not use this bridge.
guidance.developer: Add `mapping(bytes32 => bool) public processedMessages` and check/set before execution. Include the message hash in event logs for monitoring.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Flash loan functionality without invariant enforcement
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that flash loan functionality without invariant enforcement. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that flash loan functionality without invariant enforcement is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Flash loan functionality
cir_explanation.summary: Flash loan helpers (e.g., ERC-3156 or bespoke pool hooks) let users borrow the entire pool within one transaction; ensure invariants and reentrancy guards cover the borrow path.

guidance.investor: Protocols with flash loans are powerful but can be abused—prefer pools that publish invariant checks, fee schedules, and post-incident reviews.
guidance.developer: Validate `amount <= reserves`, ensure the callback cannot re-enter unsafe code, and integrate ERC-3156 style `onFlashLoan` checks with revert-on-failure semantics.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Oracle dependency without protection: No TWAP protection, No price deviation checks, No circuit breaker
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This contract relies on oracle price feeds without essential protections like time-weighted average prices (TWAP), staleness checks, or deviation limits. Flash loan attacks can manipulate spot prices within a single block to exploit price-dependent operations like borrowing, liquidations, or swaps.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Implement TWAP oracles instead of spot prices. Add staleness validation (reject prices older than X blocks). Include price deviation checks against historical values. Consider using multiple oracle sources with median aggregation.

cir_explanation.title: Oracle dependency without safeguards
cir_explanation.summary: Price feeds are consumed without TWAP smoothing, staleness checks, deviation bounds, or circuit breakers. A single-block oracle manipulation (e.g., via flash loan) could move prices enough to trigger unfair liquidations or over-borrowing.

guidance.investor: Oracle quality directly determines whether liquidations are fair and borrowing limits are safe. Missing protections mean a flash-loan attack could manipulate prices within one transaction. Check whether the team has addressed oracle security in their audit reports.
guidance.developer: For Chainlink feeds, check `updatedAt` against a maximum age and validate `answer > 0`. For Uniswap TWAP, use a sufficiently long observation window (30+ minutes). Consider fallback oracles from independent providers.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Financial operations depend on block_number - MEV risk
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This contract uses block numbers in financial calculations, which creates opportunities for miners/validators and MEV searchers to manipulate outcomes. Block-dependent logic can be exploited through transaction ordering attacks, where sophisticated actors profit at the expense of regular users. This can result in worse prices, failed transactions, or unexpected losses.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Avoid using block.number or block.timestamp for financial calculations where precision matters. If time-based logic is necessary, use commit-reveal schemes or oracle-based timestamps. Implement MEV-resistant patterns like batched auctions, time-weighted average pricing, or private mempools (Flashbots Protect). Consider the difference between 'good' MEV (arbitrage that improves prices) and 'bad' MEV (sandwich attacks).

cir_explanation.title: Time-dependent financial operations
cir_explanation.summary: Functions use `block.timestamp` for interest accrual, vesting schedules, or auction mechanics. Validators can adjust timestamps by up to ~15 seconds, which may be enough to skew time-sensitive calculations in their favour.

guidance.investor: Time-dependent logic is a moderate risk in most protocols. It becomes material when calculations are sensitive to small time differences (high-frequency interest accrual, auction deadlines, or MEV-sensitive operations).
guidance.developer: Use `block.number` for ordering-sensitive logic. For time-based calculations, add 15-second tolerance margins and ensure interest rates are not manipulable within the validator's timestamp adjustment window.

RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This bridge has multiple vulnerability classes that compound each other's risk. Cross-chain bridges custody enormous value and are prime targets for large-scale attacks — the Ronin Bridge ($625M), Poly Network ($611M), and Wormhole ($320M) hacks all exploited overlapping weaknesses. When a protocol exhibits cross-chain message validation gaps and execution control vulnerabilities, an attacker can chain these in a single transaction to bypass defences that would individually hold. The 1.3x multiplier reflects this compounding: each additional attack surface doesn't just add risk, it multiplies it.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Address both vulnerability vectors simultaneously — fixing one in isolation leaves the other exploitable, and attackers will chain them. Vectors identified: cross-chain message validation gaps and execution control vulnerabilities. Implement defence-in-depth across signature validation, message verification, validator key management, and execution controls. If any of these vectors are unclear, commission an independent security audit before deploying.

RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This lending protocol has multiple vulnerability classes that compound each other's risk. When a protocol exhibits oracle price manipulation exposure and flash-loan invariant risks, an attacker can chain these weaknesses to drain reserves in ways that individual safeguards cannot prevent. The Euler Finance ($196M) and Mango Markets ($114M) exploits both leveraged overlapping lending vulnerabilities — oracle manipulation combined with inadequate solvency checks enabled complete fund extraction. The 1.3x multiplier reflects this compounding: lending protocols with multiple exposed surfaces face multiplicative, not additive, risk.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Address both lending vulnerability vectors simultaneously — attackers will chain them. Vectors identified: oracle price manipulation exposure and flash-loan invariant risks. Implement coordinated fixes across oracle protection (TWAP, staleness checks, deviation bounds), reserve solvency verification, mint/burn access controls, and collateral health calculations. If any of these vectors are unclear, commission an independent security audit before deploying.

RiskFactors.description: External calls in bridge execution without target...
RiskFactors.full_evidence: External calls in bridge execution without target allowlist or selector gating
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that external calls in bridge execution without target allowlist or selector gating. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that external calls in bridge execution without target allowlist or selector gating is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Unbounded external call loop
cir_explanation.summary: Functions iterate over recipient arrays (e.g., `for (uint i; i < recipients.length; i++) recipients[i].call(...)`) where a malicious recipient can cause the entire batch to revert or consume all block gas.

guidance.investor: Unbounded external calls can freeze protocol operations if any recipient reverts or consumes excessive gas. This can block withdrawals, distributions, or liquidations.
guidance.developer: Replace `for` loops with push/pull patterns (allow users to claim instead of batch-sending). If looping is required, use `gasleft()` checks and `try/catch` around each external call.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Flash loan + governance with timelock protection
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This protocol's governance token can be borrowed via flash loans, which would normally be a critical risk. However, the governance system includes timelock protection, meaning any proposal must wait a delay period before execution. This gives the community time to react if an attacker attempts to pass a malicious proposal using flash-borrowed voting power. The timelock is a crucial defense - verify its duration (24+ hours is recommended).
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Good implementation of defense-in-depth. Ensure the timelock delay is meaningful (24-72 hours minimum for critical operations). Consider adding additional protections: voting snapshots from past blocks, quorum requirements, and vote-escrowed tokens that require locking. Monitor governance activity for unusual voting patterns.

cir_explanation.title: Flash loan + governance intersection
cir_explanation.summary: The protocol has both flash loan capability and governance functions. With a timelock this is manageable; without one, borrowed tokens can pass and execute proposals instantly.

guidance.investor: This protocol has both flash loan and governance features, which creates a potential attack path. Flash loans let anyone temporarily borrow huge amounts of tokens. If those tokens carry voting power, an attacker could borrow enough to pass a malicious proposal. If the protocol has a timelock (a mandatory waiting period before proposals execute), the risk is lower but still worth monitoring. Beanstalk lost $182M to exactly this attack pattern.
guidance.developer: Use ERC-20 snapshot or delegated voting locks, mandate TimelockController delays, and cap borrowed voting weight within a block.

exploit_meta.name: Makina Finance
exploit_meta.description: Flashloan oracle manipulation — permissionless updateTotalAum() with no TWAP, no deviation checks, no circuit breaker on Curve pool spot prices

sections.title: A Quiet Eureka Moment
sections.text: January 19, 2026: Makina Finance is exploited for $4.2M through a flash-loan-powered oracle manipulation attack. The Caliber vault’s updateTotalAum() function was permissionless — anyone could call it — and it read asset valuations directly from Curve pool spot prices. No TWAP. No deviation checks. No circuit breaker. The attacker flash-borrowed into the Curve pool, inflated the spot price, called updateTotalAum(), and drained the vault in a single transaction.

sections.title: How We Got Here
sections.text: When we ran the Makina contract through our CIR engine, the core attack path was already flagged — unprotected oracle dependency, permissionless execution of privileged functions, flash loans without invariant enforcement — with no new rules added. The engine’s existing rule set, calibrated through months of Time Machine training against the exploits in this collection, caught it cold. Had we been scanning the waterfront at scale, we’d have flagged it before the hack.

sections.title: What the Engine Saw
sections.text: The engine flagged unprotected oracle dependency, permissionless execution of privileged functions, and flash loan functionality without invariant enforcement. Two cluster multipliers fired: governance (1.3x) and lending (1.3x). Final score: 100/100.

sections.title: Ready for the Wild
sections.text: The Time Machine was never just a demo — it was our calibration workflow. Every exploit in this collection sharpened the rules. Every clean contract kept them honest. Makina was the moment we realised the engine had generalised: not just catching historical exploits in hindsight, but ready to detect threats in live contracts out in the wild, without any manual intervention. The same rules trained against years of DeFi exploitation history had become genuinely predictive.

breakdown.vulnerability: Flash loan oracle manipulation — unprotected price feed drained the vault
breakdown.steps: 1) Flash-borrow large position from external lending protocol. 2) Deposit into Curve pool to manipulate spot price. 3) Call permissionless updateTotalAum() on the Caliber contract — it reads the inflated Curve spot price as ground truth. 4) The inflated AUM valuation allows the attacker to withdraw more than their fair share from the vault. 5) Remove liquidity from Curve to restore the price. 6) Repay flash loan and pocket the $4.2M difference.
steps_list: Flash-borrow large position from external lending protocol
Deposit into Curve pool to manipulate spot price
Call permissionless updateTotalAum() on the Caliber contract — it reads the inflated Curve spot price as ground truth
The inflated AUM valuation allows the attacker to withdraw more than their fair share from the vault
Remove liquidity from Curve to restore the price
Repay flash loan and pocket the $4.2M difference
